| Elo max date  = 14 September 1991
| Elo min       = 162
| Elo min date  = 11 June 2017

| First game         = {{fb|FIJ|colonial}} 4–6 {{fb-rt|NZL}} <br /> ([[Suva]], [[Fiji]]; 7 October 1951)
| Largest win        = {{fb|FIJ}} 24–0 {{fb-rt|KIR}} <br /> ([[Nausori]], Fiji; 30 August 1979) <!-- The team that won 38-0 at the 2015 Pacific Games was the under 23 team, not the senior national team -->
| Largest loss       = {{fb|NZL}} 13–0 {{fb-rt|FIJ}} <br /> ([[Auckland]], [[New Zealand]]; 16 August 1981)
| Regional name      = [[OFC Men's Nations Cup|OFC Nations Cup]]
| Regional cup apps  = 9
| Regional cup first = [[1973 OFC Nations Cup|1973]]
| Regional cup best  = Third place ([[1998 OFC Nations Cup|1998]] and [[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008]])
|2ndRegional cup apps=14|2ndRegional name=[[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]|2ndRegional cup first=[[Football at the 1963 South Pacific Games|1963]]|2ndRegional cup best='''Champions''' ([[Football at the 1991 South Pacific Games|1991]],
[[Football at the 2003 South Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2003]])
|3rdRegional cup apps=10|3rdRegional name=[[MSG Prime Minister's Cup|Melanesia Cup / MSG Prime Minister's Cup]]|3rdRegional cup first=[[1988 Melanesia Cup|1988]]|3rdRegional cup best=[[Melanesia Cup]]: '''Champions''' <br />([[1988 Melanesia Cup|1988]], [[1989 Melanesia Cup|1989]], [[1992 Melanesia Cup|1992]], [[1998 Melanesia Cup|1998]], [[2000 Melanesia Cup|2000]])<br /> [[MSG Prime Minister's Cup]]: Runners-up<br />([[2024 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2024]])
| medaltemplates = {{MedalSport|Men's [[association football|football]]}}

{{Medal|3rd|[[1998 OFC Nations Cup|1998 Australia]]|}}
{{Medal|3rd|[[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008 Oceania]]|}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]}}
{{Medal|Gold|[[Football at the 1991 South Pacific Games|1991 Papua New Guinea]]|}}
{{Medal|Gold|[[Football at the 2003 South Pacific Games|2003 Fiji]]|}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 1963 South Pacific Games|1963 Fiji]]|}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 1979 South Pacific Games|1979 Fiji]]|}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 1983 South Pacific Games|1983 Western Samoa]]|}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 2007 South Pacific Games|2007 Samoa]]|}}
{{Medal|Bronze|[[Football at the 1995 South Pacific Games|1995 Tahiti]]|}}
{{Medal|Bronze|[[Football at the 2019 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2019 Samoa]]|}}
{{Medal|Bronze|[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Solomon Islands]]|}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Football at the Pacific Mini Games|Pacific Mini Games]]}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 1993 South Pacific Mini Games|1993 Vanuatu]]|}}
{{Medal|Silver|[[Football at the 2017 Pacific Mini Games – Men's tournament|2017 Vanuatu]]|}}
{{MedalComp|[[MSG Prime Minister's Cup|Melanesian Cup/MSG Prime Minister's Cup]]}}
{{Medal|W|[[1988 Melanesia Cup|1988 Solomon Islands]]|}}
{{Medal|W|[[1989 Melanesia Cup|1989 Vanuatu]]|}}
{{Medal|W|[[1992 Melanesia Cup|1992 Vanuatu]]|}}
{{Medal|W|[[1998 Melanesia Cup|1998 Vanuatu]]|}}
{{Medal|W|[[2000 Melanesia Cup|2000 Fiji]]|}}
{{Medal|RU|[[1994 Melanesia Cup|1994 Solomon Islands]]|}}
{{Medal|RU|[[2024 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2024 Solomon Islands]]|}}
{{Medal|3rd|[[1990 Melanesia Cup|1990 New Caledonia]]|}}
{{Medal|3rd|[[2022 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2022 Vanuatu]]|}}
}}

The '''Fiji men's national football team''' ({{langx|fj|timi ni soka ni Viti}}) represents [[Fiji]] in international men's [[association football]]. It is controlled by the governing body of [[football in Fiji]], the [[Fiji Football Association]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/associations/association=fij/index.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070623114612/http://www.fifa.com/associations/association=fij/index.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=23 June 2007|title=Fiji Football Association |website=fifa.com| access-date=1 February 2019}}</ref> The team plays most of their home games at the [[HFC Bank Stadium]] in [[Suva]].

Fiji first participated in the [[FIFA World Cup qualification]] in 1982; since 1990, Fiji have attempted to qualify for each World Cup without success. Their best result was a final round appearance in [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification|2010]]. The national team also represents Fiji at the [[OFC Nations Cup]], having appeared in eight out of ten previous tournaments. Fiji's best result is a third-place finish at the [[1998 OFC Nations Cup|1998]] and [[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008]] editions. They have won the [[Melanesia Cup]] five times and competed in the [[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]] from 1963 until 2015 when the competition became an under-23 tournament.

As in most countries in [[Oceania]] where football (or soccer) is not the most popular sport (such as Australia and New Zealand), Fiji's most popular sport is [[rugby union]]. As of 2022, Fiji has never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]]. In spite of this, the country's youth football team has enjoyed recent success, qualifying for both the [[2015 FIFA U-20 World Cup|2015]] and [[2023 FIFA U-20 World Cup]]s and the [[Football at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament|2016 Olympic football]] tournaments.

==History==

===Beginnings (1951–1973)===
Fiji's first international football game was against a [[New Zealand men's national football team|New Zealand]] side that was touring [[Oceania]] and had played four games against [[New Caledonia national football team|New Caledonia]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.ultimatenzsoccer.com/NZRepSoccer/id164.htm |title=List of New Zealand national soccer matches |access-date=17 September 2018 |archive-date=29 June 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160629115110/http://www.ultimatenzsoccer.com/NZRepSoccer/id164.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> The international, which took place on 7 October 1951, saw New Zealand getting the win 6-4. [[Jock Newall]] got a [[hat-trick]] for New Zealand. New Zealand returned the following year, with Fiji losing all three games, including a 9–0 drubbing in the second match.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesf/fiji-intres.html|title=Fiji – International Results|access-date=24 August 2018|archive-date=13 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813071253/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesf/fiji-intres.html|url-status=live}}.</ref>

After being absent from the international game for eleven years, the [[Fiji Football Association]] joined [[FIFA]] in 1963.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/about-fiji-fa/|title=About Fiji FA|website=fijifootball.com|access-date=24 August 2018|archive-date=26 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190426210844/https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/about-fiji-fa/|url-status=live}}</ref> That same year, the national team entered the first edition of the [[Football at the 1963 South Pacific Games|South Pacific Games]], held in Fiji. In that tournament, the country appeared in its first gold medal match after defeating Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands, but lost the match to New Caledonia 8–2. [[Marc-Kanyan Case]] scored four goals for the opposition and Fiji was relegated to silver.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac63.html|title=Football at the 1963 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=24 August 2018|archive-date=29 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221129115848/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac63.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

After missing 1966, the team's next tournament appearance was in the [[Football at the 1969 South Pacific Games|1969 Games]] held in [[Port Moresby]], Papua New Guinea. They finished in fourth place out of six teams after losing to [[Papua New Guinea national football team|Papua New Guinea]] in the bronze medal playoff.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac69.html|title=Football at the 1969 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=6 December 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221206141234/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac69.html|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Football at the 1971 South Pacific Games|Two years]] later, Fiji finished at the bottom of Group two with losses against New Caledonia and New Hebrides. They would, however, defeat the [[Cook Islands national football team|Cook Islands]] in the fifth place play-off.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac71.html|title=Football at the 1971 South Pacific Games| website=rsssf.com| access-date=29 August 2018}}</ref>

===World Cup qualifying debut and Oceania upsets (1973–1991)===
As a member of the [[Oceania Football Confederation]], Fiji played in the first edition of the [[OFC Nations Cup]] held in New Zealand in 1973. They did not win a match in the four games they played during the tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/73oc.html|title=1973 Oceania Cup|website=rsssf.com|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=30 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221130125048/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/73oc.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Competing at the [[Football at the 1975 South Pacific Games|1975 South Pacific Games]] the national team, under coach [[Sashi Mahendra Singh]], made the semi-finals of the competition where they lost to [[Tahiti national football team|Tahiti]]. In the third-place playoff, they lost to the [[Solomon Islands national football team|Solomon Islands]] by a goal.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac75.html|title=Football at the 1975 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=28 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220928221821/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac75.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

After [[John Lal]] became the new coach for Fiji in 1977, his first match as coach was an unofficial game against [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Taiwan]] which ended in a two-all draw before taking on Australia who played Taiwan because of the soccer ban in the country.<ref>{{cite web|title=Taiwan soccer ban slated|author=Laurie Schwab|newspaper=The Age|page=31|date=1976|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1300&dat=19760302&id=q_4QAAAAIBAJ&sjid=V5IDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1986,322952|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=13 April 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220413160518/https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1300&dat=19760302&id=q_4QAAAAIBAJ&sjid=V5IDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1986,322952|url-status=live}}</ref> On 19 March 1977, they took on Australia at [[ANZ National Stadium|Buckhurst Park]]. Seven national players from [[Ba F.C.]] were unavailable due to a planned tour of New Zealand. After holding off the Australians for the first forty-five minutes, [[Jimmy Okete]] (who was making his debut) scored the only goal of the game. This was a shock to the locals since the team struggled in the two tours to Australia in 1961 and 1968 against the state teams.<ref>{{cite web|title=Fiji Football To Honour 1977 Leagends|url=https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/fiji-football-to-honour-1977-leagends/|author=fijifootball|date=24 August 2017|website=fijifootball.com|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=17 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220317005949/https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/fiji-football-to-honour-1977-leagends/|url-status=live}}</ref>

Under the orders of new coach, [[Moti Musadilal]], they played some pre-tournament friendlies against New Zealand conceding nine goals in the two games before competing at the [[Football at the 1979 South Pacific Games|1979 South Pacific Games]] in [[Suva]]. After getting a nil all draw against Papua New Guinea, they scored their biggest win against [[Kiribati national football team|Kiribati]] winning by twenty-four goals. After defeating [[Wallis and Futuna national football team|Wallis and Futuna]] in the quarter-finals and Solomon Islands in the semis, they made it to their second Pacific Games final against Tahiti. In front of over twenty thousand people, Fiji came up short again with [[Erroll Bennett]] scoring a double to give Tahiti the gold medal.<ref name=SouthPacific79>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac79.html|title=Football at the 1979 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=29 August 2018|archive-date=23 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221023201629/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac79.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Fiji's next tournament was the [[1980 OFC Nations Cup]] where they were grouped with New Zealand, Solomon Islands and Tahiti. After defeating the Solomon Islands in the opening game, they were expected to lose against New Zealand who were the favourites to win the group. On 27 February 1980, they became the first Fijian team to defeat a New Zealand team with [[Dewan Chand]] and [[Meli Vuilabasa]] both scoring two goals in the victory.<ref>{{cite web |title=Four up on All Whites |url=http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=221123 |work=[[The Fiji Times]] |date=27 December 2012 |url-status=dead |archive-url=http://archive.wikiwix.com/cache/20150414000000/http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=221123 |archive-date=14 April 2015  |access-date=30 August 2018 }}</ref> Fiji did not make it to the final. They lost first to Tahiti 6–3, and then lost the third-place playoff to New Caledonia 2–1, in what was the last Oceania Cup for sixteen years.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/80oc.html|title=1980 Oceania Cup|website=rsssf.com|access-date=30 August 2018|archive-date=7 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221007024259/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/80oc.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

The following year saw a new coach with former New Zealand coach [[Wally Hughes]] leading the team into their first [[1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)|World Cup qualifiers]]. After opening with a four-goal defeat against New Zealand, they drew with [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]] nil-all, before defeating [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]] 2–1 to be in third place with three points. For Fiji that momentum was short-lived. They finished at the bottom of the group after conceding twenty-three goals in the final two games against Australia and New Zealand. Hughes resigned after the Australian game stating, "I wouldn't wish on any coach what I have been through," suggesting that bribery was involved in the defeat to Australia.<ref name=Bribery>{{cite web |title=Our darkest day |url=http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=220808 |work=[[The Fiji Times]] |date=22 December 2012 |url-status=dead |archive-url=http://archive.wikiwix.com/cache/20150414000000/http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=220808 |archive-date=14 April 2015  |access-date=30 August 2018 }}</ref>

After two years absence from international football, the national team, under [[Rudi Gutendorf]], competed at the [[Football at the 1983 South Pacific Games|1983 South Pacific Games]] in Samoa. After finishing top of the group that featured New Caledonia, Solomon Islands and Vanuatu, they defeated Papua New Guinea 2–0 in the quarter-finals before knocking off New Caledonia in the semi-final. In the final, they lost to Tahiti by a single goal which was contested by the players who attacked the referee and linesmen.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac83.html|title=Football at the 1983 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=31 August 2018|archive-date=3 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220803013601/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac83.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Soccer side attacks referee|newspaper=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|page=13|date=19 September 1983|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1301&dat=19830919&id=aP9jAAAAIBAJ&sjid=KucDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1208,7516304&hl=en|access-date=31 August 2018|archive-date=17 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220317022003/https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1301&dat=19830919&id=aP9jAAAAIBAJ&sjid=KucDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1208,7516304&hl=en|url-status=live}}</ref> Another similar incident in a friendly against New Zealand happened the following year. This led to a one-year ban of international matches being held at Fiji.<ref>{{cite web|title=Turner outed for 3 matches|newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald|page=31|date=20 December 1984|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1301&dat=19830919&id=aP9jAAAAIBAJ&sjid=KucDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1208,7516304&hl=en|access-date=31 August 2018|archive-date=17 March 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220317022003/https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1301&dat=19830919&id=aP9jAAAAIBAJ&sjid=KucDAAAAIBAJ&pg=1208,7516304&hl=en|url-status=live}}</ref>

Fiji's next tournament was the [[1988 Melanesia Cup]] held in the Solomon Islands. The national team won the final against the Solomon Islands 3–1 to claim their first title.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia88.html|title=Melanesian Cup 1988 (Solomon Islands)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=29 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220929150006/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia88.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Later in that year, they competed in the first round of the [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification – OFC first round|1990 FIFA World Cup qualification]] against Australia, with the motivation of five hundred Fijian dollars bonus for each player if they won. In front of six thousand people, they [[Fiji 1–0 Australia (1990 FIFA World Cup qualification)|defeated Australia 1–0]] with [[Ravuame Madigi]] scoring the goal to take the lead heading into the second leg.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/blog/2014/nov/11/the-forgotten-story-of-the-socceroos-defeat-to-fiji|title=The Forgotten Story of ... the Socceroos' defeat to Fiji|date=11 November 2014|website=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=25 March 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150325144929/http://www.theguardian.com/sport/blog/2014/nov/11/the-forgotten-story-of-the-socceroos-defeat-to-fiji|url-status=live}}</ref> The second leg though saw Australia take out the match by a score of 5–1 in what media called the "Battle of Newcastle". It included an all-in brawl in the last part of the match which saw [[Charlie Yankos]] get a broken nose.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nadi.com.au/nadi-news/1988/12/4/5goal-blitz-dumps-fijians/|title=5-goal Blitz Dumps Fijians|website=nadi.com.au|author=Sun Herald|date=4 December 1988|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=14 April 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150414221938/http://www.nadi.com.au/nadi-news/1988/12/4/5goal-blitz-dumps-fijians/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theage.com.au/sport/soccer/socceroos-wary-of-desperate-fiji-20020710-gdudmn.html|title=Socceroos wary of desperate Fiji|date=10 July 2002|website=theage.com|access-date=4 September 2018|publisher=[[The Age]]|archive-date=4 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180904084600/https://www.theage.com.au/sport/soccer/socceroos-wary-of-desperate-fiji-20020710-gdudmn.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

After claiming the [[1989 Melanesia Cup]] at home, in the [[1990 Melanesia Cup|following year]] they finished in third place behind Vanuatu and New Caledonia.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia89.html|title=Melanesian Cup 1989 (Fiji)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=26 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220926214725/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia89.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia90.html|title=Melanesian Cup 1990 (New Caledonia)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=9 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220809021042/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia90.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The team headed to Tahiti for the [[Football at the 1991 South Pacific Games|1991 South Pacific Games]] where they finished top of the group before defeating Vanuatu in the semi-finals. In the final, they took on the Solomon Islands where [[Radike Nawalu]] scored the opening goal of the game in the 29th minute to give Fiji the lead. The game went to penalties with Fiji winning the gold for the first time.<ref name=SouthPacifc91>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac91.html|title=South Pacific Games 1991 (Papua New Guinea)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=13 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221013133100/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac91.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Melanesia powerhouse (1991–2003)===
Fiji competed at the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|1994 FIFA World Cup qualification]] in Group B with Vanuatu and New Zealand. After losing to New Zealand in the opening game of qualifying at [[Christchurch]], they headed off to Vanuatu to compete at the [[1992 Melanesia Cup]]. They took the cup for the third time with the difference being one goal over New Caledonia.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia92.html|title=Melanesian Cup 1992 (Vanuatu)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=7 September 2018|archive-date=26 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220926225815/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia92.html|url-status=live}}</ref> After a 3–0 win over Vanuatu in Suva, they tied with New Zealand at the same ground. In the final game of the group, they defeated Vanuatu in [[Port Vila]] by the same scoreline. That game was a dead rubber as they finished behind New Zealand and were eliminated from contention.

After not qualifying for the [[1996 OFC Nations Cup]] having finished second at the [[1994 Melanesia Cup]],<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia94.html|title=Melanesian Cup 1994 (Solomon Islands)|website=rsssf.com|access-date=7 September 2018|archive-date=7 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221007065900/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/melanesia94.html|url-status=live}}</ref> at the [[Football at the 1995 South Pacific Games|1995 South Pacific Games]] in Tahiti Fiji won the bronze medal by defeating Vanuatu in the bronze medal playoff.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac95.html|title=1995 South Pacific Games|website=rsssf.com|access-date=7 September 2018|archive-date=9 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221009225707/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac95.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Attempting to qualify for the [[1998 FIFA World Cup]], Fiji got a bye into the second round of qualifying where they were paired with New Zealand and Papua New Guinea. The top team qualified for the third round. The nation finished in second place with losses to New Zealand both at home (1–0) and away (5–0) being the critical factors in their elimination. Fiji's next tournament was the [[1998 OFC Nations Cup]] after they qualified by taking out the [[1998 Melanesia Cup]] held in Vanuatu. The only blemish being a one–all draw against the Solomon Islands.<ref name=Oceania98>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98oc.html|title=Oceanian Nations Cup 1998|website=rsssf.com|access-date=9 September 2018|archive-date=31 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221031173822/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/98oc.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

After losing to the hosts (Australia) 3–0 in their first game, they defeated the [[Cook Islands national football team|Cook Islands]] 3–1 to secure a semi-final spot where they fell to New Zealand by a single goal. This put them into the third-place playoff where they defeated Tahiti for their best result to date at the [[OFC Nations Cup]] with a third-place. [[Esala Masi]] was the top goalscorer for the tournament with three goals.<ref name=Oceania98/> Fiji qualified for the [[2000 OFC Nations Cup|following edition]] of the Nations Cup after winning the title at the [[2000 Melanesia Cup]] in Fiji.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/00oc.html|title=Oceanian Nations Cup 2000|website=rsssf.com|access-date=9 September 2018|archive-date=11 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220711004747/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/00oc.html|url-status=live}}</ref> But later that year, Fiji withdrew because of political unrest in the country, which led to the [[2000 Fijian coup d'état]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=138862|title=Official cheers as Fiji fixtures dry up|author=Bronwyn, Sell|date=30 June 2000|access-date=9 September 2018|publisher=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|archive-date=9 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180909073801/https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=138862|url-status=live}}</ref>

Fiji's [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|qualification]] for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup]] started at [[Coffs Harbour]] when they were grouped with American Samoa, Australia, Samoa and Tonga in Group one. In the opening game of the group, they defeated American Samoa 13–0 to record their biggest win in a FIFA match. [[Shailemdra Lal]] scored five goals; [[Esala Masi]] scored four.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.espnfc.com/worldcup/news/2001/0407/20010407fiji.html|title=Fiji open qualifying with 13 goals|date=7 April 2001|access-date=11 September 2018|publisher=[[ESPN]]|archive-date=3 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303165231/http://www.espnfc.com/worldcup/news/2001/0407/20010407fiji.html|url-status=live}}</ref> After defeating Samoa 6–1, they lost to Australia 2–0 to finish in second place and were eliminated from contention. Only the top team from the group qualified through to the second round.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.espnfc.com/worldcup/news/2001/0414/20010414wcupaussie.html|title=Australia beat Fiji to top qualifying group|date=14 April 2001|access-date=11 September 2018|publisher=[[ESPN]]|archive-date=11 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180911081945/http://www.espnfc.com/worldcup/news/2001/0414/20010414wcupaussie.html|url-status=live}}</ref> They met Australia again at the [[2002 OFC Nations Cup]] in New Zealand. They finished in third place in the group after only winning against New Caledonia followed by an 8–0 loss against Australia. This eliminated Fiji from the semi-finals and saw the sack of [[Billy Singh]] ending a seven-year reign in his third stint as coach of the national team.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theage.com.au/sport/soccer/a-good-knight-for-socceroos-20020711-gdudr4.html|title=A good Knight for Socceroos|date=11 July 2002|access-date=11 September 2018|publisher=[[The Age]]|archive-date=11 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180911114355/https://www.theage.com.au/sport/soccer/a-good-knight-for-socceroos-20020711-gdudr4.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Fiji claimed the [[Football at the 2003 South Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2003 South Pacific Games]] gold medal at home. The squad, which consisted mostly of young players, was able to go unbeaten throughout the tournament defeating New Caledonia in the gold medal match. [[Esala Masi]] was Fiji's top scorer for Fiji with twelve goals which included the seven in the 12–0 win over Kiribati in the group stage.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://fr.fifa.com/live-scores/news/y=2003/m=7/news=les-fidji-couronnees-reines-pacifique-sud-88185.html|title=Les Fidji couronnées reines du Pacifique Sud|work=FIFA.com |date=21 July 2003|access-date=11 September 2018|language=fr|publisher=[[FIFA]]|archive-date=11 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180911114249/https://fr.fifa.com/live-scores/news/y=2003/m=7/news=les-fidji-couronnees-reines-pacifique-sud-88185.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac03.html|title=South Pacific Games 2003 (Fiji)|date=20 August 2015|website=rsssf.com|access-date=11 September 2018|archive-date=8 February 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230208031236/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac03.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Recent times (2004–)===
Fiji's qualification for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup]] started in Samoa. They finished in second place in the qualifying group which featured Vanuatu, Papua New Guinea, Samoa and American Samoa. Their only loss was against Vanuatu on the final match-day.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://websites.sportstg.com/assoc_page.cgi?client=1-2674-0-0-0&sID=25179&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=75413|title=Stage 2 teams for Adelaide confirmed|date=27 May 2004|website=websites.sportstg.com|access-date=14 September 2018|archive-date=14 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180914094457/http://websites.sportstg.com/assoc_page.cgi?client=1-2674-0-0-0&sID=25179&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=75413|url-status=live}}</ref> This meant that Fiji qualified through to the [[2004 OFC Nations Cup]]. After drawing with Tahiti and getting a win over Vanuatu, their World Cup chance  ended when Fiji lost the next three matches and were eliminated at the second stage of qualifying.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/04oc.html|title=Oceanian Nations Cup 2004|date=9 October 2008|website=rsssf.com|access-date=14 September 2018|archive-date=11 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220711004746/https://www.rsssf.org/tables/04oc.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Fiji's next tournament was the [[Football at the 2007 South Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2007 South Pacific Games]] in Samoa, which was also the first round of [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|qualifying]] for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]] in South Africa. After getting safely through the group, which included Tuvalu, Tahiti, Cook Islands and New Caledonia, they took on Vanuatu for a spot in the semi-finals to qualify for the next round.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/fiji-top-vanuatu-through-587894|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180914132104/https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/fiji-top-vanuatu-through-587894|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2018|title=Fiji on top, Vanuatu through|date=4 September 2007|access-date=14 September 2018|publisher=[[FIFA]]}}</ref> In the semi-finals, they defeated Vanuatu to advance to the second round of qualifying with a three-goal victory, confirming their spot in the gold medal match. They lost by a single goal to New Caledonia, however, to finish runner-up for the fourth time.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/joy-for-new-caledonia-and-vanuatu-590264|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180914132000/https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/joy-for-new-caledonia-and-vanuatu-590264|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2018|title=Joy for New Caledonia and Vanuatu|date=8 September 2007|access-date=14 September 2018|publisher=[[FIFA]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac07.html|title=South Pacific Games 2007 (Samoa)|date=20 August 2015|website=rsssf.com|access-date=14 September 2018|archive-date=13 August 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813184720/https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/southpac07.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/ofc-five-hit-new-highs-600271|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180914132051/https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/ofc-five-hit-new-highs-600271|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2018|title=OFC five hit new highs|date=17 September 2007|access-date=14 September 2018|publisher=[[FIFA]]}}</ref>

The second round of qualifying got off to a rocky start. Goalkeeper, [[Simione Tamanisau]] was refused entry because his father had been  linked to the [[2006 Fijian coup d'état]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/7040180.stm|title=Fiji match pulled over visa row|date=12 October 2007|access-date=14 September 2018|publisher=[[BBC]]|archive-date=14 October 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071014005308/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/asia-pacific/7040180.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> The match was moved to the end of the qualifying period with Fiji winning 2–0. During that reschedule, the national team played the rest of their World Cup games. The team finished in third place in the group after getting only one point in their first three games—a three-all draw against New Caledonia. This was a key factor as the later 4–0 loss to the same team ended their hopes for qualifying to the next stage.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/new-dawn-oceania-642011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180914132013/https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/new-dawn-oceania-642011|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2018|title=A 'new' dawn in Oceania|date=21 November 2007|access-date=14 September 2018|publisher=[[FIFA]]}}</ref>

Under the guidance of [[Gurjit Singh (football manager)|Gurjit Singh]], the team competed at the [[Football at the 2011 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2011 Pacific Games]] in New Caledonia. In the group stage, the team qualified for the semi-finals with a game to spare as they would go through with a clean sweep of wins.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-16803/|title=Fiji march into semi-finals|date=3 September 2011|access-date=16 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=16 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916130133/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-16803/|url-status=live}}</ref> In the semi-finals, they took the lead against the Solomon Islands in the sixty-ninth minute of play with a goal by [[Maciu Dunadamu]]. But goals from [[Joses Nawo]] and [[Henry Fa'arodo]], however, gave the Solomon Islands the win and a spot in the gold medal match. Fiji finished in fourth place losing to Tahiti in the bronze medal match 2–1.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-7161/|title=Hosts win through to men's and women's finals|date=8 September 2011|access-date=16 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=16 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916130145/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-7161/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-10779/|title=Tahiti and Fiji earn bronze medals|date=9 September 2011|access-date=16 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=16 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916093433/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-10779/|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[Juan Carlos Buzzetti]] returned to Fiji's top job as he led the team to the [[2012 OFC Nations Cup]]. This was also the second part of qualifying for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=187329|title=Buzzetti returns to guide Fiji|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150414113653/http://www.fijitimes.com/story.aspx?id=187329 |archive-date=14 April 2015|access-date=16 September 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref> Competing in Group B, they lost the opening game to New Zealand. The only goalscorer of the match was Kiwi player [[Tommy Smith (footballer, born 1990)|Tommy Smith]] in the eleventh minute. After a nil-all draw against the Solomon Islands, they got their only goal of the tournament with [[Maciu Dunadamu]] opening the scoring against Papua New Guinea in the fifteenth minute. But a late goal from [[Kema Jack]] ended the tournament for Fiji with a draw and knocked them out in the group stage.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-6123/|title=Tight battles define second day|date=2 June 2012|access-date=16 September 2018|archive-date=16 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916093402/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-6123/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-7825/|title=NZ through as hosts and Fiji draw blanks|date=4 June 2012|access-date=16 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=16 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180916093451/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-7825/|url-status=live}}</ref>

After not competing in a senior international for three years, the national team had two friendlies against A-League side [[Wellington Phoenix FC|Wellington Phoenix]] before entering their under-23 squad for the [[2015 Pacific Games]] in Papua New Guinea. This was also a qualifier for the [[2016 Summer Olympics]] in Brazil. After losing the opening game, they got a draw in the second match with [[Roy Krishna]] and [[Ilimotama Jese]] each scoring a goal for Fiji.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-6945/|title=Fiji sides test Phoenix|date=29 June 2015|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917071441/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-6945/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.wellingtonphoenix.com/news/match-report-honours-shared-fiji-airways-tour-finale|title=Honours Shared in Fiji Airways Tour Finale|date=1 July 2015|website=wellintonphoenix.com|access-date=17 September 2018|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917105202/https://www.wellingtonphoenix.com/news/match-report-honours-shared-fiji-airways-tour-finale|url-status=live}}</ref> More friendlies took place before the national team headed to Papua New Guinea for the [[2016 OFC Nations Cup]]. This was also the second round of [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (OFC)|qualification]] for the 2018 World Cup. With a squad that only had three outside of the National League, Fiji lost the opening game against New Zealand with Krishna getting the only goal in the 3–1 loss.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-9389/|title=All Whites and Solomons off the mark|date=29 May 2016|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917071356/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-9389/|url-status=live}}</ref> After defeating the Solomon Islands in their second game with a Krishna goal, they needed to at least draw Vanuatu in the final group game to have a chance at qualifying to the semis.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-17457/|title=All Whites in driving seat|date=1 June 2016|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917071417/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-17457/|url-status=live}}</ref> After almost getting the early lead from Krishna, Fiji fell behind by two goals from Dominique Fred and [[Fenedy Masauvakalo]] giving Vanuatu the lead. The second half saw Fiji tie the match at two-all as goals from [[Samuela Kautoga]] and Krishna gave the team hope of qualifying. But Fiji was eliminated from the competition as a penalty in the 75th minute by [[Brian Kaltack]] gave Vanuatu a spot in the semis by goal difference.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-14121/|title=New Zealand take full points into semis|date=5 June 2016|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917071405/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/archives-14121/|url-status=live}}</ref>

For finishing third place, they qualified through to the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – OFC third round|2018 FIFA World Cup qualification third round]]. Fiji was drawn to face New Zealand and New Caledonia in Group A. Before the first game against New Zealand, [[Frank Farina]] was replaced by [[Christophe Gamel]] in the top job with Gamel stating, "We respect our opponents and we know that they are strong" in his first press conference as manager.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/fiji-appoint-new-coach/|title=Fiji appoint new coach|date=27 January 2017|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=22 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170222053643/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/fiji-appoint-new-coach/|url-status=live}}</ref> Fiji only got a single point from their four games. That point was against New Caledonia at [[Lautoka]] with the game ending in a two–all draw.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/fiji-fight-back-to-catch-new-caledonia/|title=Fiji fight back to catch New Caledonia|date=7 June 2017|access-date=17 September 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=17 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180917071429/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/fiji-fight-back-to-catch-new-caledonia/|url-status=live}}</ref>

In July 2025, Fiji was invited by [[Football Association of Thailand]] to compete in the [[2025 King's Cup]] held in September 2025.

== Team image ==

===Kit sponsorship===
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:left;"
|-
! Kit lier
! Period
|-
|align="Left"|{{flagicon|England}} [[Umbro]] ||1981–???? <ref>{{Cite web|url = https://www.oldfootballshirts.com/pt/teams/f/fiji/old-fiji-football-shirt-s22656.html|title = Fiji camisa de futebol 1980 – 1984|date = 27 February 2011|access-date = 4 April 2020|archive-date = 8 October 2017|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171008080943/http://www.oldfootballshirts.com/pt/teams/f/fiji/old-fiji-football-shirt-s22656.html|url-status = live}}</ref>

|-

|-

|-

|}

=== Home stadium ===
{{main|HFC Bank Stadium}}
[[File:ANZ Stadium Fiji.jpg|thumb|[[HFC Bank Stadium]] in Suva, Fiji, hosts all of Fiji national matches.]]
The first soccer international held in Fiji was held at [[Albert Park (Suva)|Albert Park]], when New Zealand took on the hosts in 1951. The stadium, the main centre in Fiji during the early 20th century, was also the home of cricket and of the country's first [[Rugby union]] test match in 1926.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/about-fiji-fa/|title=ABOUT FIJI FA|website=fijifootball.com|access-date=17 September 2018|archive-date=26 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190426210844/https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/about-fiji-fa/|url-status=live}}</ref>

In modern times, most games hosted by Fiji are held at the [[HFC Bank Stadium]], which was built in 1951 and has been renovated twice which can hold a capacity of 15,000. The first renovation was in 1978 for the [[1979 South Pacific Games]] held in Suva.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.foxsportspulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?client=1-2689-0-0-0&sID=121642&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=8634422 |access-date = 3 June 2014 | title = South Pacific Games 1979 – Fiji |work=Solomon Islands Games Record |publisher=Fox Sports Pulse |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303194222/http://www.foxsportspulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?client=1-2689-0-0-0&sID=121642&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=8634422 |archive-date=3 March 2016 }}</ref> A second renovation took place in 2012 sponsored by [[ANZ Fiji]] at a cost 17.5&nbsp;million Fijian dollars in exchange for the stadium's naming rights.<ref>{{cite news |title=$17.5m For Stadium Upgrade |date=9 August 2012 |first=Anasilini |last=Ratuva |newspaper=[[Fiji Sun]] |url=http://fijisun.com.fj/2012/08/09/17-5m-for-stadium-upgrade/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160426033904/http://fijisun.com.fj/2012/08/09/17-5m-for-stadium-upgrade/ |archive-date=26 April 2016}}</ref> The football team has also played games at [[Lautoka]], [[Ba (town)|Ba]], [[Nausori]] and [[Labasa]].<ref name=SouthPacific79/><ref>{{cite web|url=https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2008/09/06/oceania/wc-qualifying-oceania/fiji/vanuatu/522132/|title=International Friendly: Fiji vs. Vanuatu|access-date=19 November 2018|date=6 September 2008|publisher=soccerway|archive-date=19 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181119053057/https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2008/09/06/oceania/wc-qualifying-oceania/fiji/vanuatu/522132/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.oceaniafootball.com/competition-list/2018-fifa-world-cup-russia%E2%84%A2-preliminary-competition-oceanian-zone--round-3-group-a-400546/fiji-new-zealand-337055/|title=2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – Fiji vs. New Zealand|date=25 March 2017|access-date=19 November 2018|publisher=[[Oceania Football Confederation]]|archive-date=6 September 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180906090459/https://www.oceaniafootball.com/competition-list/2018-fifa-world-cup-russia%E2%84%A2-preliminary-competition-oceanian-zone--round-3-group-a-400546/fiji-new-zealand-337055/|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Results and fixtures==
{{See also|Fiji national football team results (2000–present)}}
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

{{legend2|#CCFFCC|Win|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFFFCC|Draw|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFCCCC|Loss|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFFFFF|Fixture|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}

}}
{{footballbox collapsible
| format     = 1
| round      = [[2025 MSG Prime Minister's Cup – Men's tournament#Semi-finals|2025 MSG Prime Minister's Cup semi-final]]

| team2      = {{fb|FIJ}}

| result     = L
}}

}}

==Current technical staff==
{| class=wikitable
|-
! colspan="2"|Coaching Staff
|-
| Head coach || {{flagicon|GLP|football}} [[Stéphane Auvray]]<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.fijifootball.com.fj/auvray-appointed-as-fiji-national-mens-football-team-head-coach/ |publisher=Fiji Football Association|date=20 October 2025|title=Auvray Appointed as Fiji National Men’s Football Team Head Coach}}</ref>
|-
| Technical Director || {{flagicon|FRA}} [[David Baltase]]<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.fbcnews.com.fj/sports/fiji-fa-appoints-french-expert-as-new-technical-director/ |publisher=FBC News|date=4 September 2025|title=Fiji FA appoints French expert as new technical director}}</ref>
|-
| Team Manager || {{flagicon|FIJ}} Kartik Reddy
|-
| Assistant coach || {{flagicon|FIJ}} Marika Rodu
|-
| Goalkeeping coach || {{flagicon|FIJ}} Sanaila Waqanicakau
|-
|Physiotherapist ||{{flagicon|IND}} Noel Augustine
|-
|}

===Coaching history===
[[File:Frank Farina cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|[[Frank Farina]] became the manager of the national football team of Fiji in 2015]]
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{Flagicon|FIJ|colonial}} [[Sashi Mahendra Singh]] (1960–1972)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Mohammed Ali Sahu Khan]] (1972–1974)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Sashi Mahendra Singh]] (1974–1976)
*{{flagicon|FIJ}} [[John Lal]] (1977–1978)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Moti Musadilal]] (1979)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Sashi Mahendra Singh]] (1980)
*{{flagicon|NZL}} [[Wally Hughes]] (1981–1982)
*{{Flagicon|FRG}} [[Rudi Gutendorf]] (1983)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Michael Thoman]] (1983–1984)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Billy Singh]] (1985–1986)
*{{Flagicon|FRG}} [[Rudi Gutendorf]] (1987)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Billy Singh]] (1987–1992)
*{{Flagicon|SCO}} [[Danny McLennan]] (1993–1995)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Billy Singh]] (1995–2002)
*{{Flagicon|AUS}} [[Les Scheinflug]] (2002)
*{{Flagicon|AUS}} [[Tony Buesnel]] (2003–2004)
*{{Flagicon|AUS}} [[Lee Sterrey]] (2005–2006)
*{{Flagicon|URU}} [[Juan Carlos Buzzetti]] (2006–2009)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Yogendra Dutt]] (2009–2010)
*{{Flagicon|ARG}} [[Claudio Canosa]] (2011)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Gurjit Singh (footballer)|Gurjit Singh]] (2011)
*{{Flagicon|URU}} [[Juan Carlos Buzzetti]] (2011–2015)
*{{Flagicon|AUS}} [[Frank Farina]] (2015–2016)
*{{Flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Christophe Gamel]] (2016–2019)
*{{Flagicon|DNK}} [[Flemming Serritslev]] (2020–2022)
*{{Flagicon|FIJ}} [[Marika Rodu (football coach)|Marika Rodu]] (2023)
*{{flagicon|WAL}} [[Rob Sherman (football manager)|Robert Sherman]] (2023–2025)
*{{flagicon|GLP|football}} [[Stéphane Auvray]] (2025–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following 23 players were called up for the [[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]].<ref>{{cite web |title=The DIGICEL Fiji Men's National Extended Squad has been called up for the National Camp next month in preparation for the OFC Nations Cup 2024|url=https://x.com/FijiFootball_/status/1783172552107688442|website=Twitter|date=24 April 2024|publisher=Fiji Football Association}}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of 22 June 2024, after the game against [[Tahiti national football team|Tahiti]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=1|pos=GK|name=[[Aydin Mustahib]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|5|28|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Manurewa AFC|Manurewa]]|clubnat=NZL}}
{{nat fs g player|no=20|pos=GK|name=[[Joji Vuakaca]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|3|24|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Labasa F.C.|Labasa]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=22|pos=GK|name=[[Isikeli Sevanaia]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|1|11|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=[[Rewa F.C.|Rewa]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs break}}

{{nat fs g player|no=3|pos=DF|name=[[Gabriele Matanisiga]]|age={{birth date and age|1995|6|14|df=y}}|caps=13|goals=2|club=[[Bula FC (Fiji)|Bula FC]]|clubnat=FIJ}}

{{nat fs g player|no=18|pos=DF|name=[[Lekima Gonerau]]|age={{birth date and age|1989|12|8|df=y}}|caps=7|goals=0|club=[[Labasa F.C.|Labasa]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=21|pos=DF|name=[[Sterling Vasconcellos]]|age={{birth date and age|2005|4|19|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=0|club=[[Bula FC (Fiji)|Bula FC]]|clubnat=FIJ}}

{{nat fs g player|no=5|pos=MF|name=[[Sitiveni Cavuilagi]]|age={{birth date and age|1994|7|26|df=y}}|caps=15|goals=0|club=[[Lautoka F.C.|Lautoka]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=6|pos=MF|name=[[Thomas Dunn (footballer, born 2003)|Thomas Dunn]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|1|19|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=6|club=[[Bula FC (Fiji)|Bula FC]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=7|pos=MF|name=[[Mohammed Raheem]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|8|10|df=y}}|caps=4|goals=2|club=[[Ba F.C.|Ba]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=8|pos=MF|name=[[Setareki Hughes]]|age={{birth date and age|1995|6|8|df=y}}|caps=41|goals=3|club=[[Bula FC (Fiji)|Bula FC]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=10|pos=MF|name=[[Nabil Begg]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|3|17|df=y}}|caps=12|goals=4|club=[[Bula FC (Fiji)|Bula FC]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=11|pos=MF|name=[[Brendan McMullen]]|age={{birth date and age|2002|6|30|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=[[Stop Out Sports Club|Stop Out]]|clubnat=NZL}}
{{nat fs g player|no=12|pos=MF|name=[[Tevita Waranaivalu]]|age={{birth date and age|1995|9|16|df=y}}|caps=24|goals=2|club=[[Rewa F.C.|Rewa]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=13|pos=MF|name=[[Mosese Nabose]]|age={{birth date and age|1998|7|1|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Tailevu Naitasiri F.C.|Tailevu Naitasiri]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g player|no=19|pos=MF|name=[[Merrill Nand]]|age={{birth date and age|2000|9|22|df=y}}|caps=6|goals=1|club=[[Suva F.C.|Suva]]|clubnat=FIJ}}

{{nat fs g player|no=23|pos=FW|name=[[Rusiate Matarerega]]|age={{birth date and age|1993|1|17|df=y}}|caps=18|goals=4|club=[[Nadi F.C.|Nadi]]|clubnat=FIJ}}
{{nat fs g end}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have also been called up to represent Fiji in the last 12 months:

{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Atunaisa Naucukidi]]|age={{birth date and age|1997|8|3|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Ba F.C.|Ba]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]]}}<sup>PRE</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Akuila Mateisuva]]|age={{birth date and age|1992|1|15|df=y}}|caps=11|goals=0|club=[[Suva F.C.|Suva]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Mohammed Alzaar Alam]]|age={{birth date and age|2000|2|8|df=y}}|caps=5|goals=0|club=[[Rewa F.C.|Rewa]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]]}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Epeli Lairoti]]|age={{birth date and age|1995|6|3|df=y}}|caps=7|goals=0|club=[[Suva F.C.|Suva]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]]}}<sup>PRE</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Kishan Sami]]|age={{birth date and age|2000|3|13|df=y}}|caps=19|goals=1|club=[[Rewa F.C.|Rewa]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Ilimotama Jese]]|age={{birth date and age|1990|3|16|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=0|club=[[Lautoka F.C.|Lautoka]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]]}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Dave Radrigai]]|age={{birth date and age|1990|3|15|df=y}}|caps=29|goals=2|club=[[Suva F.C.|Suva]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]]}}<sup>PRE</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[William Valentine (footballer)|William Valentine]]|age={{birth date and age|df=yes|1997|10|11}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Nadi F.C.|Nadi]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]]}}<sup>PRE</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Mohammed Ramzan Khan]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|11|22|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Ba F.C.|Ba]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[2024 OFC Men's Nations Cup]]}}<sup>PRE</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Patrick Joseph (footballer)|Patrick Joseph]]|age={{birth date and age|1998|5|3|df=y}}|caps=19|goals=2|club=[[Rewa F.C.|Rewa]]|clubnat=FIJ|latest=[[Football at the 2023 Pacific Games – Men's tournament|2023 Pacific Games]]}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby
*<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team
*<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew due to non-injury issue
{{nat fs end}}

|Third place||3rd||5||3||2||0||19||6
|-style="background:gold;"

|- style="background:silver;"
|{{flagicon|SAM}} [[Men's football at the 2007 South Pacific Games|2007]]

|-

|Third place||3rd||4||2||1||1||14||4
|-style="background:#CC9966;"

==Best / Worst Results==
Source:<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te14209/fiji/ | title=Fiji }}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.worldfootball.net/teams/te14209/fiji/ | title=Fiji - Historical results }}</ref>

* [[Fiji national football team results (1951–1999)]]
* [[Fiji national football team results (2000–present)]]

===Best===
# 24–0 vs. Kiribati, 1979
# 16–0 vs. Tuvalu, 2007
# 15–1 vs. Cook Islands, 1971
# 14–1 vs. Guam, 1991
# 13–0 vs. American Samoa, 2001
# 12–0 vs. Guam, 1993
# 12–0 vs. Kiribati, 2003
# 11–0 vs. Guam, 1975
# 11–0 vs. American Samoa, 2004
# 10–0 vs. Solomon Islands, 1983
# 10–0 vs. Northern Mariana Islands, 2023
# 10–1 vs. Tuvalu, 2019

===Worst===
# 0–13 vs. New Zealand, 1981
# 0–11 vs. New Caledonia, 1969
# 0–10 vs. Australia, 1981
# 0–9 vs. New Zealand, 1952
# 0–8 vs. Australia, 2002

==Honours==
===Continental===
*'''[[OFC Nations Cup]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (2): [[1998 OFC Nations Cup|1998]], [[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008]]

===Regional===
*'''[[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (2)''': [[1991 South Pacific Games|1991]], [[2003 South Pacific Games|2003]]
** {{Silver2}} Silver medal (4): [[1963 Pacific Games|1963]], [[1979 South Pacific Games|1979]], [[1983 Pacific Games|1983]], [[2007 Pacific Games|2007]] 
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (3): [[1995 South Pacific Games|1995]], [[2019 Pacific Games|2019]], [[2023 Pacific Games|2023]]
* '''[[MSG Prime Minister's Cup|Melanesia Cup / MSG Prime Minister's Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (5)''': [[1988 Melanesia Cup|1988]], [[1989 Melanesia Cup|1989]], [[1992 Melanesia Cup|1992]], [[1998 Melanesia Cup|1998]], [[2000 Melanesia Cup|2000]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1994 Melanesia Cup|1994]], [[2024 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2024]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (2): [[1990 Melanesia Cup|1990]], [[2022 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2022]]

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
|-
!Competition !!{{gold1}} !!{{silver2}} !!{{bronze3}} !!Total
|-
|align=left|[[OFC Men's Nations Cup|OFC Nations Cup]]
|0||0||2||2
|-
!Total!!0!!0!!2!!2
|}
